sanskrit: सर्वभूतानि कौन्तेय प्रकृतिं यान्ति मामिकाम् । कल्पक्षये पुनस्तानि कल्पादौ विसृजाम्यहम् ॥
hindiTranslation: हे कुन्तीनन्दन  कल्पोंका क्षय होनेपर सम्पूर्ण प्राणी मेरी प्रकृतिको प्राप्त होते हैं और कल्पोंके आदिमें मैं फिर उनकी रचना करता हूँ ॥
englishTranslation: O son of Kunti, at the end of a cosmic cycle all beings enter my nature; at its beginning, I send them forth again.
essence: What seems like ending is only a return before another beginning.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: At the end of a vast cycle, every being dissolves back into Krishna's nature. When the next cycle begins, he brings them forth again.
simpleInsight: You are held inside a larger order, even when everything seems to end.
detailedMeaning: On the Kurukshetra battlefield, Arjuna is frozen by the scale of loss. Krishna widens the frame beyond one war: worlds themselves rise and dissolve in cycles, and every being is gathered back and sent out again. That cosmic rhythm steadies Arjuna's panic.
modernRelevance: A project collapses, a relationship ends, a role disappears — and it feels final. This verse pulls the mind back from that panic and shows that endings belong to a larger cycle, not absolute loss.
translationLiteral: सर्वभूतानि (all beings) / कौन्तेय (O son of Kunti) / प्रकृतिं (nature) / यान्ति (go to) / मामिकाम् (my) / कल्पक्षये (at the end of a cosmic cycle) / पुनः (again) / तानि (them) / कल्पादौ (at the beginning of a cosmic cycle) / विसृजामि (I send forth) / अहम् (I)
insight: It shifts attention from personal control to the larger rhythm that carries all change.

quoteVariants.text: All beings return to my nature; I send them forth again.
All beings return. All beings rise.
